IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem—learning across multiple heterogeneous Chinese segmentation criteria—and proposes concrete shared-private architectures plus adversarial training to enforce criterion-invariant features. Empirical results on eight SIGHAN corpora show averaged F-measure improvements and useful cross-script transfer (simplified → traditional), indicating the approach can meaningfully improve multi-criteria CWS. However, gains are sometimes small or not significant and shared parameters can hurt large datasets without adversarial mitigation, suggesting moderate rather than transformative impact and limited immediate disruption of prevailing practices. | B(3): The paper addresses an important practical problem—learning across heterogeneous Chinese segmentation criteria—by proposing adversarial multi-criteria learning and three shared-private neural architectures and evaluating them on eight SIGHAN corpora. It demonstrates measurable F-measure improvements and useful transfer (shared features from simplified Chinese helping traditional Chinese datasets), which could encourage researchers to adopt multi-criteria and adversarial shared-private designs. However, reported gains from the adversarial component are sometimes small and shared parameters can hurt performance on some large datasets, limiting the work's immediate transformative impact and suggesting a moderate overall influence.

However, gains are sometimes small or not significant and shared parameters can hurt large datasets without adversarial mitigation, suggesting moderate rather than transformative impact and limited immediate disruption of prevailing practices.

It demonstrates measurable F-measure improvements and useful transfer (shared features from simplified Chinese helping traditional Chinese datasets), which could encourage researchers to adopt multi-criteria and adversarial shared-private designs. However, reported gains from the adversarial component are sometimes small and shared parameters can hurt performance on some large datasets, limiting the work's immediate transformative impact and suggesting a moderate overall influence.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP task (Chinese word segmentation) and proposes multi-criteria/adversarial learning methods that are of clear interest to top NLP/ML venues. It uses standard, state-of-the-art architectures (Bi-LSTM+CRF), evaluates on widely used SIGHAN corpora with F-measure, and compares appropriate baselines (single-criterion and stacked Bi-LSTM). The multi-corpus/transfer and adversarial components target the multi-task/multi-criteria audience in the community, so the topic, audience, formulation, methods, and evaluation are all well aligned with a top-tier venue. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (Chinese word segmentation) using an established and relevant modeling paradigm (Bi-LSTM+CRF with shared-private architectures and adversarial training) that is squarely within top-tier conference scope. The experimental setup and evaluation are appropriate for the venue: experiments on eight SIGHAN corpora (MSRA, PKU, CTB, etc.) with F-measure comparisons against Bi-LSTM and single-criterion baselines match community standards. The formulation (multi-criteria learning, three shared-private architectures, criterion discriminator with entropy objective) and related work coverage are positioned correctly for the NLP/ML research audience.

It uses standard, state-of-the-art architectures (Bi-LSTM+CRF), evaluates on widely used SIGHAN corpora with F-measure, and compares appropriate baselines (single-criterion and stacked Bi-LSTM). The multi-corpus/transfer and adversarial components target the multi-task/multi-criteria audience in the community, so the topic, audience, formulation, methods, and evaluation are all well aligned with a top-tier venue.

The experimental setup and evaluation are appropriate for the venue: experiments on eight SIGHAN corpora (MSRA, PKU, CTB, etc.) with F-measure comparisons against Bi-LSTM and single-criterion baselines match community standards. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies known ideas (shared-private architectures and adversarial domain-invariant feature learning) to the well-studied task of Chinese word segmentation and proposes three concrete shared-private variants plus an entropy-based adversarial objective to learn criterion-invariant features. While the specific combination and the application to multi-criteria CWS (with experiments on eight SIGHAN corpora and cross-script transfer claims) are useful, the core techniques are non-novel and represent an incremental, rather than fundamentally new, methodological contribution. | B(3): The paper applies known ideas (shared-private multi-task architectures and adversarial training to enforce task-invariant features) to Chinese word segmentation and presents three architectural variants plus an entropy-based adversarial objective. Designing parallel/stacked/skip-layer shared-private Bi-LSTM structures and showing empirical gains across eight SIGHAN corpora is useful engineering work, but the core methods (shared-private decomposition and adversarial discriminator) are established techniques. Overall this is an incremental, moderate-novelty contribution rather than a fundamentally new idea.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies known ideas (shared-private architectures and adversarial domain-invariant feature learning) to the well-studied task of Chinese word segmentation and proposes three concrete shared-private variants plus an entropy-based adversarial objective to learn criterion-invariant features. While the specific combination and the application to multi-criteria CWS (with experiments on eight SIGHAN corpora and cross-script transfer claims) are useful, the core techniques are non-novel and represent an incremental, rather than fundamentally new, methodological contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies known ideas (shared-private multi-task architectures and adversarial training to enforce task-invariant features) to Chinese word segmentation and presents three architectural variants plus an entropy-based adversarial objective. Designing parallel/stacked/skip-layer shared-private Bi-LSTM structures and showing empirical gains across eight SIGHAN corpora is useful engineering work, but the core methods (shared-private decomposition and adversarial discriminator) are established techniques. Overall this is an incremental, moderate-novelty contribution rather than a fundamentally new idea.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides reasonably comprehensive experiments across eight SIGHAN corpora and compares multiple baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM, single-criterion) while evaluating three shared-private architectures and the effect of adversarial training, which serves as useful ablations. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and the authors discuss limitations (shared-parameter bias hurting some large datasets and small/insignificant adversarial gains). However, important reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters or code mentioned) and there is limited sensitivity analysis of hyperparameters or statistical significance testing, so a few important gaps remain. | B(4): Experiments are reasonably comprehensive: evaluation across eight SIGHAN corpora with comparisons to Bi-LSTM baselines, stacked Bi-LSTM, and single-criterion learning, and exploration of three shared-private architectures plus adversarial vs. non-adversarial training. The paper reports some failure modes and limitations (shared-parameter bias, noisy features, small adversarial gains) and thus discusses negative results. However, important reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are missing and sensitivity/ablation analyses appear limited beyond the three architecture choices, leaving minor gaps in depth and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides reasonably comprehensive experiments across eight SIGHAN corpora and compares multiple baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM, single-criterion) while evaluating three shared-private architectures and the effect of adversarial training, which serves as useful ablations. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and the authors discuss limitations (shared-parameter bias hurting some large datasets and small/insignificant adversarial gains). However, important reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters or code mentioned) and there is limited sensitivity analysis of hyperparameters or statistical significance testing, so a few important gaps remain.

reviewer_b.justification: Experiments are reasonably comprehensive: evaluation across eight SIGHAN corpora with comparisons to Bi-LSTM baselines, stacked Bi-LSTM, and single-criterion learning, and exploration of three shared-private architectures plus adversarial vs. non-adversarial training. The paper reports some failure modes and limitations (shared-parameter bias, noisy features, small adversarial gains) and thus discusses negative results. However, important reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are missing and sensitivity/ablation analyses appear limited beyond the three architecture choices, leaving minor gaps in depth and reproducibility.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates the proposed models against reasonable neural baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM, and single-criterion learning) across eight SIGHAN corpora using F-measure, so comparisons are performed on the same data and metrics and include analysis of when shared parameters hurt and how adversarial training helps. It also studies three shared-private architectures, showing clear contrasts with baselines. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to other prior multi-criteria or state-of-the-art segmentation approaches beyond these neural baselines, and some experimental details (e.g., hyperparameters) are not reported, so a few relevant baselines or conditions may be missing. | B(4): The paper compares against reasonable and relevant baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM, and single-criterion learning) using the same SIGHAN corpora and F-measure metric, so the comparisons appear fair. It also analyzes differences between the proposed shared/private architectures and the effect of adversarial training, and it acknowledges limitations and cases where gains are small or can hurt large datasets. However, the evaluation seems to omit direct comparisons to other prior multi-criteria or multi-task CWS approaches (a minor but notable omission), so the comparison coverage is good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates the proposed models against reasonable neural baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM, and single-criterion learning) across eight SIGHAN corpora using F-measure, so comparisons are performed on the same data and metrics and include analysis of when shared parameters hurt and how adversarial training helps. It also studies three shared-private architectures, showing clear contrasts with baselines. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to other prior multi-criteria or state-of-the-art segmentation approaches beyond these neural baselines, and some experimental details (e.g., hyperparameters) are not reported, so a few relevant baselines or conditions may be missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against reasonable and relevant baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM, and single-criterion learning) using the same SIGHAN corpora and F-measure metric, so the comparisons appear fair. It also analyzes differences between the proposed shared/private architectures and the effect of adversarial training, and it acknowledges limitations and cases where gains are small or can hurt large datasets. However, the evaluation seems to omit direct comparisons to other prior multi-criteria or multi-task CWS approaches (a minor but notable omission), so the comparison coverage is good but not fully comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The proposed architectures and adversarial objective are reasonable and experimentally evaluated on eight SIGHAN corpora, and the paper reports averaged F-measure improvements versus single-criterion baselines. However, key methodological details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance testing or available code), and the adversarial benefit is reported as small or not significant in some cases. There are no indicated mathematical derivations or proofs to verify, and while the adversarial entropy objective is plausible, direct evidence that shared features become criterion-invariant is limited. Overall the main claims are partially supported but there are notable gaps in experimental rigor and evidence. | B(4): The main claims (multi-criteria shared-private architectures improve per-corpus CWS and adversarial training encourages criterion-invariant features) are supported by experiments across eight SIGHAN corpora and comparisons to reasonable baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM, single-criterion models). The use of a Bi-LSTM+CRF backbone and an entropy-maximization adversarial objective is technically sound and no incorrect derivations are reported. However, important experimental details are missing (hyperparameters, code) and no mention of statistical significance testing is made, and the paper itself notes small or negative effects on some large datasets—minor issues that weaken but do not invalidate the conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The proposed architectures and adversarial objective are reasonable and experimentally evaluated on eight SIGHAN corpora, and the paper reports averaged F-measure improvements versus single-criterion baselines. However, key methodological details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance testing or available code), and the adversarial benefit is reported as small or not significant in some cases. There are no indicated mathematical derivations or proofs to verify, and while the adversarial entropy objective is plausible, direct evidence that shared features become criterion-invariant is limited. Overall the main claims are partially supported but there are notable gaps in experimental rigor and evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims (multi-criteria shared-private architectures improve per-corpus CWS and adversarial training encourages criterion-invariant features) are supported by experiments across eight SIGHAN corpora and comparisons to reasonable baselines (Bi-LSTM, stacked Bi-LSTM, single-criterion models). The use of a Bi-LSTM+CRF backbone and an entropy-maximization adversarial objective is technically sound and no incorrect derivations are reported. However, important experimental details are missing (hyperparameters, code) and no mention of statistical significance testing is made, and the paper itself notes small or negative effects on some large datasets—minor issues that weaken but do not invalidate the conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a logical flow (intro → model descriptions of three shared-private architectures → adversarial training → experiments on eight SIGHAN corpora → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (multi-criteria learning + three architectures + adversarial objective). Notation and presentation are generally clear and related work coverage is adequate, and the experimental tables/examples appear informative given the reported per-corpus results. Minor presentation issues reduce clarity for reproduction: hyperparameters are not reported, and the adversarial improvements are described as small or not significant in some cases, making the practical impact harder to assess. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a logical flow from motivation to method (three shared-private architectures and adversarial training) to experiments on eight SIGHAN corpora. The main contributions are stated early and are easy to identify (adversarial multi-criteria learning and three architecture designs). Notation and methodological description (Bi-LSTM+CRF, shared vs. private layers, discriminator and entropy objective) are consistent and clear enough to follow, and datasets and baselines are clearly listed. Writing quality is described as adequate and related work coverage acceptable, though omission of reported hyperparameters and no comment in the summary about figures/tables slightly reduce presentation completeness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a logical flow (intro → model descriptions of three shared-private architectures → adversarial training → experiments on eight SIGHAN corpora → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (multi-criteria learning + three architectures + adversarial objective). Notation and presentation are generally clear and related work coverage is adequate, and the experimental tables/examples appear informative given the reported per-corpus results. Minor presentation issues reduce clarity for reproduction: hyperparameters are not reported, and the adversarial improvements are described as small or not significant in some cases, making the practical impact harder to assess.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a logical flow from motivation to method (three shared-private architectures and adversarial training) to experiments on eight SIGHAN corpora. The main contributions are stated early and are easy to identify (adversarial multi-criteria learning and three architecture designs). Notation and methodological description (Bi-LSTM+CRF, shared vs. private layers, discriminator and entropy objective) are consistent and clear enough to follow, and datasets and baselines are clearly listed. Writing quality is described as adequate and related work coverage acceptable, though omission of reported hyperparameters and no comment in the summary about figures/tables slightly reduce presentation completeness.

main_claims: Proposes adversarial multi-criteria learning for Chinese word segmentation to integrate shared knowledge from multiple heterogeneous segmentation criteria and improve per-corpus performance compared to single-criterion learning.

Designs three shared-private neural architectures (Model-I: parallel shared-private, Model-II: stacked shared-private, Model-III: skip-layer shared-private) to separate criterion-invariant and criterion-specific features.

Introduces adversarial training (a criterion discriminator and an entropy-maximization objective on shared features) to encourage the shared layer to learn criterion-invariant features, further improving segmentation results.

Demonstrates via experiments on eight SIGHAN corpora that multi-criteria learning yields averaged F-measure improvements and that shared features from simplified Chinese can help traditional Chinese datasets.
_summary.methodology: Use a Bi-LSTM+CRF sequence labeling model with a shared embedding layer and a feature layer composed of a shared Bi-LSTM and per-criterion private Bi-LSTMs in three sharing architectures; train jointly across M corpora with a CRF log-likelihood objective plus adversarial objectives where a criterion discriminator is trained and the shared parameters are trained to maximize the entropy of predicted criterion distribution.
limitations: Shared parameters can bias toward other segmentation criteria and introduce noisy features, hurting performance on some large datasets (e.g., MSRA, AS) unless adversarial training is used.
Improvements from the adversarial strategy are reported as not significant in some cases (boost is small).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - Chinese word segmentation (multi-criteria / multi-task learning)
